planting.note: Start from cuttings or established plants 4–6 weeks before last frost; Origanum syriacum is the plant known as za'atar in Middle Eastern cooking and as Biblical hyssop (aizov) in Hebrew, the foundational herb of the Middle Eastern za'atar spice blend and one of the most widely used herbs in Lebanese, Syrian, Palestinian, and Israeli cuisine.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings (seeds are slow and variable) and plant in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky soil in full sun; Origanum syriacum is native to rocky limestone hills of the eastern Mediterranean and requires excellent drainage — it declines in heavy or wet soil. Origanum syriacum is the 'true za'atar' plant of the Middle East — the primary ingredient in the za'atar spice blend that is ubiquitous in Levantine cuisine (the blend also includes sesame seeds, sumac, and salt). It is sometimes called Biblical hyssop because many scholars believe it is the ezov/aizov of the Hebrew Bible. The flavor is more intense, pungent, and earthy than Italian oregano with a distinctive mineral-herb complexity that makes it unique among Mediterranean herbs; harvest by cutting stems before full flowering for maximum flavor. Hardy only to zone 7; treat as an annual or pot plant in colder climates. In warm zones 8–11 it can be grown as a perennial hedge-shrub.
sources: Israeli Institute for Technology — Za'atar Research

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, enriched soil; Buttercup is a classic Cucurbita maxima winter squash with a distinctive button-like cap on the blossom end, dark green to grey-green skin, and dense, dry, sweet orange flesh — for decades the standard of flavor comparison for all winter squash and still considered one of the finest for table use.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in hills or rows after last frost when soil is above 65°F; thin to 2 plants per hill and allow 5–8 feet between hills. Buttercup squash was introduced by Harris Seeds of Rochester, New York in 1932 and became the gold standard for winter squash flavor for much of the twentieth century; a Cucurbita maxima type with a distinctive flattened-round shape, dark green skin with grayish-green stripes, a small, warty 'button' (the remnant of the blossom end), and dense, dry, sweet, deep-orange flesh that is exceptionally dry and rich — much less watery than kabocha or acorn types. The sweet, nutty, slightly dry flesh is outstanding for mashing, baking, and pies; it stores for 2–3 months. The modern 'kabocha' squash type is very similar in form and is often confused with Buttercup; true Buttercup is larger and has the distinctive button. Cure for 10 days at room temperature before storage.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for a spring crop; Deer Tongue is an American heirloom lettuce dating to at least 1740, listed in Thomas Jefferson's Monticello garden journal, with distinctively tongue-shaped, slightly succulent leaves in a loose, non-heading rosette — one of the most heat-tolerant lettuces for spring gardens.

directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface in early spring or fall in fertile, moist, well-drained soil; thin to 9–12 inches for full rosettes or 6 inches for cut-and-come-again harvest. Deer Tongue (also known as Matchless) is one of America's oldest heirloom lettuces, documented in seed catalogs since at least 1740 and recorded in Thomas Jefferson's Monticello garden journals; the distinctive tongue-shaped, elongated, slightly pointed, wavy-edged leaves form loose, open rosettes with a crisp-yet-buttery texture and mild, sweet flavor. A green variety ('Green Deer Tongue') and a bronze or red variant ('Bronze Deer Tongue') exist; both are more heat-tolerant than most lettuce types, slower to bolt in warm conditions, and excellent for spring and fall production in warm climates. The elongated leaf shape is immediately distinctive and visually striking in the garden; makes beautiful salad contributions for the unusual leaf form.
sources: Monticello Heritage Harvest Festival Seed List

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, enriched soil; Eight Ball is an AAS winner round zucchini producing billiard-ball-shaped, dark green, glossy fruits ideal for stuffing whole — an outstanding novelty-and-culinary squash for the summer garden that produces prolifically on compact bush plants.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in fertile, well-drained, enriched soil; Eight Ball is a compact bush type that needs 2–3 feet of space but no support. Eight Ball is an All-America Selections award-winning round zucchini producing compact, perfectly spherical, dark green fruits 2–4 inches in diameter — essentially billiard ball-shaped summer squash with the same mild, tender, slightly sweet flavor as regular zucchini but in a form perfect for stuffing whole. Hollow them out (a melon baller works perfectly), fill with cheese, rice, meat, and herbs, and bake for a complete stuffed vegetable presentation; they hold their round shape beautifully when baked. Harvest at 2–3 inches for perfect stuffing size; larger fruits become seedy and tougher. Highly productive on compact, bushy plants; good for small-space and container gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring harvest, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before first fall frost; pak choi (bok choy) is the quintessential cool-season Asian green, harvested as a whole plant or as individual outer leaves, with crisp, succulent stems and tender, mild leaves that are fundamental in Chinese stir-fry and soup cooking.

directions: Direct sow 1/2 inch deep in spring or late summer in fertile, moist, well-drained soil; thin to 6–12 inches depending on desired head size. Pak choi (Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis) is the quintessential Chinese white-stemmed leafy vegetable grown throughout East Asia for thousands of years and now foundational in Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and Southeast Asian cooking; the distinctive appearance — a rosette of smooth, deep-green leaves on broad, crisp, white to pale green, fleshy stems — is immediately recognizable. Both the leaves and stems are edible and have different textures: the stems are crisp, juicy, and mild; the leaves are tender and slightly more bitter. Used in stir-fries, soups (pho, hot pot, won ton), dumplings, and braised dishes throughout Asian cuisines. Bolts quickly in heat but is excellent as a fall-through-spring crop in zones 8–11. Baby pak choi (harvested at 3–4 inches) is increasingly popular for microgreens and market sales.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension Asian Vegetables
Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide
